excerpt: Islam comprises two inseparable dimensions: ʿibādāt (worship) and muʿāmalāt (conduct and social dealings). The first connects us with our Creator; the second binds us to His creation.
This world is not the place of reckoning but of trial. In it, the obedient and disobedient alike may prosper materially, for Allah does not assume the posture of judge—rather, that of witness.
source_location_hint: On the nature of the present world and divine justice in the hereafter

excerpt: According to the Sulemani tradition, Islam encompasses seven pillars: wilāyah, ṭahārah, salāt, zakāt, sawm, hajj, and jihād. Though the hadith does not explicitly name these three—wilāyah, ṭahārah, and jihād—they are implicit within the five mentioned, not contradictory to them.
source_location_hint: On the seven pillars as implicit extensions of the five explicit foundations

excerpt: Wilāyah derives from wa-li-ya, signifying spiritual affinity, intimacy, deep friendship. A derivative, mawlā (master, guardian), denotes the one who possesses authority and control.
source_location_hint: Conceptual treatment of wilāyah and mawlā

excerpt: The verse revealed on the Day of Ghadīr speaks of three realities: the perfection of the faith, the completion of blessings, and divine contentment with Islam. Not only has the religion been perfected on this day—Allah's grace is now complete, and the Almighty is satisfied with the submission of this community.
source_location_hint: Exegesis of the Day of Ghadīr and the perfection of the faith

excerpt: The Messenger proclaimed: I am the city of knowledge, and ʿAlī is its gate. Whoever desires to enter the city must enter through the gate. The gate guards the city, preserves it. ʿAlī is thus the gateway to prophetic wisdom.
source_authority: Ismaili preacher (dāʿī) and contemporary of the Fatimid state
source_location_hint: On ʿAlī as the gate to the Prophet's knowledge

excerpt: After proclaiming 'Whomsoever considered me his mawlā, from this moment onwards, ʿAlī is his mawlā,' the Messenger invoked: O Allah! Be near to those who are near to him; be an enemy to those who are his enemies; aid those who aid him; abandon those who abandon him. And turn truth in the direction that he turns!
source_location_hint: Doctrinal affirmation of wilāyah through the Day of Ghadīr

excerpt: Prayer derives from sīlah, meaning to join with, to link together. For this connection nourishes love and enables it to grow. The more one connects with the Beloved, the more the intensity of love increases.
source_location_hint: On prayer as connection and spiritual nourishment

excerpt: Fasting is the most difficult form of worship, for the believer must abandon his desire for sustenance out of love for Allah. Allah indicates: I will reward it according to My discretion; I will multiply its reward as I wish. For fasting is an act of patience (sabr).
source_authority: Ismaili esoteric exegesis of Islamic law
source_location_hint: On the spiritual meaning and reward of fasting

excerpt: Salmān al-Fārisī traveled the world seeking the Messenger of Allah. He became so close to the Messenger that the Prophet said: Salmān is from among my family. Such is the closeness that love brings!
source_location_hint: On exemplars of spiritual love and devotion

excerpt: The lover rushes toward the Beloved, crying out: Here I am, O Allah, here I am! Here I am; there is no partner with You; here I am! Truly, all praise and all blessing belong to You, and sovereignty—You have no partner in it.
source_location_hint: On the talbiyah and the essence of ḥajj as spiritual longing

excerpt: When Junayd al-Baghdādī heard the young girl's verses expressing her love for Allah and heard her profound critique of merely external worship, he lost consciousness. When he awoke, the girl had vanished. This exemplifies the overwhelming power of sincere ʿishq.
source_location_hint: On the annihilation of the self in the love of Allah

excerpt: Hajj is waṣāl (union), liqāʾu-l-maḥbūb (the meeting of lover and Beloved). It represents the culmination of the spiritual journey from recognition through steadfast bonding to passionate love to ultimate union.
source_authority: Early Ismaili esotericism and spiritual anthropology
source_location_hint: On the metaphysical meaning of ḥajj within the hierarchy of souls

excerpt: There was an outward migration (hijrah) that the Messenger performed when he journeyed from Mecca to Medina. That physical hijrah stands as a sign and reminder that every believer must also make a spiritual hijrah—moving from sin to obedience, from disobedience to submission, from evil to good.
source_location_hint: On the spiritual significance of migration from worldliness to submission
